seeds.title: What You're Telling Your Child When You Track Them at Camp
target_keywords: letting kids have independence at camp
should I monitor my child at camp
seeds.hook: The app sends you a photo every few hours. A location ping when they cross into the dining hall. You can see, in real time, whether they're smiling. And you check it constantly — because what if they're not okay? But here's the question underneath that question: what is it like to be a child who is always being watched for signs that they need rescuing?
seeds.alreadyloved_angle: The camp surveillance trend (Slate, May 2026: parents using facial recognition + GPS tracking at sleepaway camp) is exploding. Every other parenting site is treating it as a logistics/safety debate. Nobody is writing about what the watching communicates to the child. AlreadyLoved angle: a child who is constantly monitored learns that they are not trusted to be okay without you watching. Their security comes from your surveillance, not from within. The alternative isn't abandonment — it's the radical act of sending them with something sturdier than a location pin: the knowledge that they belong to you regardless of whether you can see them.
seeds.trend_source: Slate May 2026 — summer camp monitoring trend; camp enrollment season peak
